title: Bhagavad Gita Introduction and Concepts
focus: Introduces the structure of the Gita and its core philosophical concepts like Dharma, Karma, Jnana, and Bhakti.
rationale: Fallback section derived from packet-level grouping.
sectionExcerpt:
[Page 029]
# Manuscript Collection of Bhagavadgītā (Successfully inscribed in 2025)\n\n* The Bhagavadgītā, containing 700 verses in 18 chapters, is embedded within the Bhīṣmaparva (adhyāya 23-40) of the epic Mahābhārata.\n* It takes the form of a dialogue between Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna with armies lined up for the great war intended to free Arjuna of dejection (viṣāda).

[Page 030]
Ancient Epic Dialogue
Part of the Mahabharata's Bhishma Parva; a profound conversation between Lord Krishna and warrior Arjuna on the Kurukshetra battlefield.
Structure & Dating
Comprises 700 verses across 18 chapters, composed around the 2nd or 1st century BCE.
Philosophical Synthesis
Masterfully synthesises Vedic, Buddhist, Jain, and Charvaka philosophies into a unified wisdom tradition.
Core Concepts of Bhagavad Gita:
- Dharma: Righteous duty and moral path.
- Karma: The law of action and consequence.
- Jnana: Knowledge and self-realisation.
- Bhakti: Devotional love and surrender.
evidenceBullets:
# Manuscript Collection of Bhagavadgītā (Successfully inscribed in 2025)

* The Bhagavadgītā, containing 700 verses in 18 chapters, is embedded within the Bhīṣmaparva (adhyāya 23-40) of the epic Mahābhārata.
* It takes the form of a dialogue between Kṛṣṇa and Arjuna with armies lined up for the great war intended to free Arjuna of dejection (viṣāda).
Part of the Mahabharata's Bhishma Parva; a profound conversation between Lord Krishna and warrior Arjuna on the Kurukshetra battlefield.
Comprises 700 verses across 18 chapters, composed around the 2nd or 1st century BCE.
